coredata.dc:title: Selective and sensitive fluorescence turn-on detection of bilirubin using resorcinol-sucrose derived carbon dot
coredata.dc:creator: Barik, Balaram
Mohapatra, Sasmita
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have been prepared from resorcinol and sucrose (rsCDs) hydrothermally, which show green emission at 525 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 17.2%. The intense emission of rsCDs is quenched upon the addition of Cu2+. In the presence of bilirubin (BR), the emission intensity is enhanced due to the competitive binding of Cu2+ with bilirubin and hence releasing rsCDs to the sensing medium. It is the first time report on turn-on fluorescence sensing towards BR with a detection limit of 85 nM. Even in the presence of other comparable biomolecules, the sensor is selective and ultrasensitive to bilirubin. A cellulose paper-based sensor strip has also been designed for the naked-eye detection of BR in blood serum. Due to the specific recognition of this rsCDs towards BR, it can be applied to detect BR in practical human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Turn-on sensor
Bilirubin
Paper-strip sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of bilirubin in real sample
Optimization of rsCD-Cu2+ probe and detection of free bilirubin
Effect of pH and response time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of rsCDs, inset HRTEM showing [012] plane, (b) STEM-EDAX showing atomic percentage of elements, (c) X-ray diffractogram of rsCDs, (d) Raman spectrum of rsCDs.

(a) Comparison of UV–Visible spectra of resorcinol-sucrose precursor (rsCP) and rsCDs, (b) Emission spectra of rsCDs at various excitation wavelength, (c) pH sensitiveness of rsCDs at 440 nm excitation, (d) emission spectra of rsCDs with respect to time.

Change in FL spectra of rsCDs with addition of Cu2+, (b) Stern-Volmer plot showing linear range for decrease in concentration of rsCDs with Cu2+ (λex 440 nm), (c) Enhancement in fluorescence intensity of rsCDs-Cu2+ in presence of bilirubin, (d) increase in relative FL intensity with respect to bilirubin concentration.

(a) Selective increase in FL intensity of rsCDs-Cu(II) probe in presence of bilirubin compared to other biomolecules, (b) Increase in FL intensity of bilirubin in presence of other biomolecules indicating no interference from other biomolecules in the sensing process.
XPS (a) C1s (b) O1s (c) Cu2p (d) Fluorescence life time of rsCD and rsCD-Cu.

(a) Schematic presentation of fluorescence quenching of rsCDs probe on addition of Cu2+, (b) change in color of rsCDs dispersion on addition of Cu2+ and followed by bilirubin, (c) detection of bilirubin using paper based sensor strip.
Comparison of performance based on fluorescence detection of bilirubin by other quantum dot based method.

row.entry: Gold nanocluters over indium tin oxide
gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) based Ratiometric film sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery percentage of bilirubin assayed using rsCD-Cu2+ sensor.

row.entry: Found by our method after spiking μM

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data obtained following the present assay method.

head.ce:title: Selective and sensitive fluorescence turn-on detection of bilirubin using resorcinol-sucrose derived carbon dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, 769008, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology, Rourkela, 769008, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have been prepared from resorcinol and sucrose (rsCDs) hydrothermally, which show green emission at 525 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 17.2%. The intense emission of rsCDs is quenched upon the addition of Cu2+. In the presence of bilirubin (BR), the emission intensity is enhanced due to the competitive binding of Cu2+ with bilirubin and hence releasing rsCDs to the sensing medium. It is the first time report on turn-on fluorescence sensing towards BR with a detection limit of 85 nM. Even in the presence of other comparable biomolecules, the sensor is selective and ultrasensitive to bilirubin. A cellulose paper-based sensor strip has also been designed for the naked-eye detection of BR in blood serum. Due to the specific recognition of this rsCDs towards BR, it can be applied to detect BR in practical human serum samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Bilirubin (BR) is a strong yellow pigment that results from heme catabolism. Hyperbilirubinemia, persistent jaundice, Crigler-Najjar syndrome, encephalopathy, and Dublin-Johnson syndromes are all associated with aberrant BR levels [1,2]. Free unconjugated BR and glucuronic acid conjugated BR constitute the total serum bilirubin [3]. The total serum bilirubin in a healthy human is less than 25 μM, but in jaundice conditions, bilirubin concentration is greater than 50 μM. The bilirubin level in hyperbilirubinimic conditions is above 170 μM and 340 μM in adults and newborns, respectively [4]. However, it exceeds 425 μM for critical neonatal hyperbilirubinemia where unconjugated bilirubin, after crossing the blood-brain barrier may cause a neurological disorder called Kernicterus [5]. Consequently, accurate determination of total bilirubin is increasingly becoming important because of its enormous importance in adult as well as immediate neonatal management.

The Jendrassik-Grof (J-G) method is adopted to determine bilirubin in blood serum in usual pathological practice. J-G method forms an azo dye based on the reaction of BL with diazotized sulfanilic acid, which is red in neutral and blue in alkaline medium [6]. In this process, the turnaround time from sample collection to verification is lengthy and requires expert technicians. Further, using so many chemicals increases the possibility of error in the estimation. The J-G method's practical application is also limited by the possibility of interference from other heme proteins and the diazo reaction's pH dependency sensitivity [7]. Various other spectroscopic methods [8–10], electrochemical [11–14], and liquid chromatographic methods [15–17] have been published subsequently. Among a few spectroscopic techniques, cellulose strips decorated with novel graphene-isolated-Au-nanocrystals [18] and Au-decorated Si-nanowires for Surface Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy [19,20] are eye-catching. Among few reported fluorescence assays, the polyfluorene-based method is well cited. Due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer from polyfluorene to bilirubin, bilirubin coupled with polyfluorene via glucuronic acid causes a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of polyfluorene [21]. Recently, quantum dot-based fluorescence detection has been an active method due to its high sensitivity, easy operational procedure, and affordability. Zhao et al. have reported HSA modified QDs (QDs-HSA) as a biorecognition element to detect free bilirubin by forming the QDs-HSA-FB complex [22]. This complex forms aggregates on PEI polymer nanoparticles which can be easily identified by imaging. Abha et al. have reported l-cysteine tailored manganese doped zinc sulphide quantum dots for accurate determination of total serum bilirubin where bilirubin acts as an electron donor and Mn: ZnS QD acts as an electron acceptor [23]. Borse et al. recently announced lysozyme-synthesized fluorescent gold and molybdenum bimetallic nanoclusters (Au–Mo NCs) for bilirubin sensing with a LoD of 28.26 nM [24]. Xiao et al. reported using the Langmuir-Blodgett (LB) process to fabricate an ultrathin film of glucuronic acid-functionalized gold nanoclusters to detect bilirubin with a limit of detection 0.27 nM [25]. Although these quantum dot-based detection processes selectively sense bilirubin, the working mechanism is essentially based on fluorescence quenching-based detection. In fluorescence detection, turn-on sensor is more preferable to a turn-off sensor, as small amounts of light are measured by it compared to a dark background. Alternatively, a decade ago, carbon dots (CDs) emerged as a potential alternative material against heavy metal-based quantum dots owing to their excellent photostability, biocompatibility and tuneable emission wavelength. Their outstanding performances fused with facile synthesis from readily available and cheap precursors provide a solid foundation for extensive applications of these nano lights in bioimaging, biosensing, and medical diagnosis [26]. CDs are especially organic materials as many hydrophilic functional groups such as –OH and COOH on their surface, making CDs relatively soluble in water and more appropriate for biological samples than intrinsically toxic, inorganic fluorescent quantum dots. Several fluorescence sensors have been designed for several biomolecules such as glucose [27], glutathione [28], alkaline phosphate [29], lysine [30], cholesterol [31], Vit B-12 [32], and many more as discussed by Liu et al. [33]. However, despite such noticeable properties, there is no consistent report on the application of CDs in the recognition and quantification of total serum bilirubin.

This paper reports the design of CDs from resorcinol and sucrose (rsCDs) following hydrothermal synthesis. The as prepared rsCDs show excellent green luminescence (λem = 525 nm) under 440 nm excitation. The addition of Cu2+ to the aqueous solution of rsCDs results in fluorescence quenching because of nonradiative energy transfer during the rsCDs−Cu2+ complex formation process. In the presence of bilirubin, the rsCDs−Cu2+ complex disassembles due to competitive binding between carbon dot and tetrapyrrole group of bilirubin. In this process, the fluorescence intensity of rsCDs is regained, thus achieving reliable turn-off-on sensing towards serum bilirubin. In comparison to other expensive metal nanoparticle-based methods for the detection of bilirubin [34], the present process shows selectivity, a meagre limit of detection (LOD) of 85 nM and efficiently works in the linear range of concentration (0.7–3.6 μM). As a result, it has been successfully used to detect bilirubin in actual samples like blood serum of healthy people, jaundice patients, and newborn babies.

Resorcinol was purchased from fisher scientific. Sucrose, CuSO4.5H2O, urea, and absolute ethanol were purchased from Merck. Bilirubin, glucose, and glutathione were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Uric acid was purchased from NICE chemicals. Millipore water (18.2 MΩ) has been used for all the experiments.

Resorcinol (0.5 g) and sucrose (1.7 g) were dissolved in 50 ml of Millipore water and 20 ml of absolute ethanol. Then, the transparent solution was put in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After the end of the reaction, a yellowish-brown solution was obtained. The reaction mixture was extracted in dichloromethane (DCM) and water mixture in a separating funnel. The DCM layer was discarded as it contained unreacted organic impurities. The obtained aqueous layer was centrifuged at 9000 and the supernatant was collected. Further, the supernatant was centrifuged at 60000 rpm using ultra-centrifuge (HITACHI, CS120FNX) and the brown colour precipitate was collected. The residue was then dialyzed for 24 h against Millipore water with a dialysis membrane of 1000 Da. Finally, the yellow-coloured rsCDs do residue was produced. Lyophilization was used to recover the rsCDs.

50 μL of rsCDs solution (5 mg/mL), 100 μL Cu(II) (80 mM) were mixed in water. Then BR solution was prepared by adding 1 mg bilirubin to 15 ml distilled water. Then 40 μL of 1 M NaOH was added to dissolve it and to make its volume to 20 ml, added 4.96 ml of distilled water. Then the different volume of bilirubin solution of the same concentration was added and the solution was incubated at 15 °C for 30 min. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded at λex = 440 nm. To investigate the selectivity, some similar interfering biomolecules such as glucose, glutathione, urea, uric acid, glucosamine, fructose, bisphenol A and hemoglobin were also tested at the same concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of bilirubin in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed bioassay's potential utility is illustrated by its use to detect bilirubin in human serum. Three blood samples with the known bilirubin concentration were collected from the nearest hospital, including serum from healthy people, jaundice patients, and newborn babies. All the serum samples were diluted 90 times with water. A particular concentration of bilirubin was spiked into diluted serum samples. The bilirubin concentration was assayed after adding Cu2+ following the protocol described below.

A Horiba Quantamaster fluorimeter was used to acquire photoluminescence spectra. Using an integrating sphere, a Quantamaster (QM-400, PTI-Horiba) measured the absolute quantum yield. The calculations had been done using Felix software. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI Technai G2 30ST) operating at 300 kV was used to perform microstructural investigation and elemental mapping. The presence of a surface functional group was studied using an IRAffinity-1S Shimadzu spectrophotometer to record FTIR spectra. XPS was used to verify the surface composition of rsCD using the AlKα excitation source in the PHI 5000 Versa Prob II (FEI Inc) instrument. The Nano ZS 90, Malvern instrument was used to quantify particle size and mean zeta potential at various pH levels.

The reaction between resorcinol and sucrose can be proposed as follows (Scheme 1 ). The triple dehydration of the monosaccharide leads to 5-hydroxy methylfurfural (ketohexoses) or to furfural (ketopentoses) [35]. Subsequently, the furan derivative reacts with resorcinol to give the coloured condensation product called the Seliwanoff product [36]. Soluble polymers are then aromatized and condensed via keto-enol tautomerization or intramolecular dehydration to form aromatic clusters. At critical supersaturation point the nucleation of particles is triggered to give rsCDs. Diffusion helps the generated nuclei to develop and conjugation with traces unreacted precursors on the surface of the particle results in a functional CDs [37].

The hydrodynamic size of the carbon quantum dots was measured by DLS and confirmed by TEM. The hydrodynamic size of the rsCDs was found to be 24 nm as shown in Fig. S1a. The TEM image indicated uniform rsCDs with diameters ranging from 2 to 7 nm (Fig. 1 a). The hydrodynamic size of rsCDs was higher as compared to TEM size. The large size of rsCDs in an aqueous medium could be explained by the Coulombic interaction of particles forming clusters. The high-resolution TEM image showed a lattice spacing of 0.4 nm, which was in agreement with the [012] graphite plane (Fig. 1a, inset). The STEM-EDAX (Fig. 1d) indicated that rsCDs are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen. The XRD pattern of the synthesized rsCDs showed an intense peak at 2θ = 22.4° which could be assigned to the (002) diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1c). The Raman spectrum of rsCDs showed two well defined D-band at 1350 cm−1 and a G band at 1582 cm−1 (Fig. 1d). The G-band (peak at 1582 cm−1) corresponded to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice, while the D-band (peak at around 1350 cm−1) corresponded to the vibration of carbon atoms in the plane with dangling bonds. The ID/IG intensity ratio in our case is 1.07, which indicates a more amorphous nature of rsCDs. The characteristic bandwidth at 3325 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of rsCDs indicated the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces (Fig. S1b). C–H stretching has a peak at 2920 cm−1, while the CO stretching frequency of the –COOH, –CO, and –CHO groups has a high peak at 1730 cm−1. The peak at 1620 cm−1 is assigned to the CC stretching frequencies. The surface charge of rsCDs was determined by measuring zeta potential over pH range 2–11. It was found that the magnitude of negative charge on the surface of the carbon quantum dot was increased with an increase in pH because of the formation of carboxylate groups on the surface (Fig. S1c).

To investigate the formation of rsCDs, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of rsCDs was compared with that of the resorcinol-sucrose precursor (rsCP). In the case of the rsCDs, clear redshifts were observed in both higher and lower energy band in comparison to rsCP, which signifies the presence of low energy traps in rsCDs. In addition, a new band appears at a higher wavelength at 476 nm, which demonstrates the existence of surface energy traps in rsCDs (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence (FL) of the rsCDs has been investigated systematically. With an excitation wavelength 440 nm, intense emission at 525 nm was observed. When an aqueous solution of rsCDs was excited within the range of 400 nm to 520 nm, a strong emission resulted within the range of 490 nm to 615 nm (Fig. 2b). The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was calculated by an absolute method using an integrating sphere. The PLQY was found to be 17% (λ ex 440 nm, λ em 525 nm) which was comparable to the PLQY of CDs reported by other research groups. The fluorescence intensity of rsCD increases when pH increases from 3 to 10 (Fig. 2c), justifying rsCDs as an excellent pH sensor at the higher excitation wavelength (476 nm). The increase in FL intensity of rsCDs can be ascribed to the surface –COOH and phenolic OH groups, which get deprotonated at alkaline pH, thus forming more no of delocalized π bonds and also contributing more nonbonding electrons to the CDs, which enhances FL. Furthermore, the intensity remained nearly unaltered after 12 h of UV exposure, indicating that the rsCD is highly photostable (Fig. 2d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of rsCD-Cu2+ probe and detection of free bilirubin
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of the rsCDs (5 mg/ml, pH 6.5) probe towards different concentrations of Cu2+ has been recorded at λ ex 440 nm. Upon adding of Cu2+, a gradual decrease in the emission intensity at 524 nm was clearly seen, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of the rsCDs was significantly quenched by Cu2+ (Fig. 3 a). A linear relationship (R2 = 0.99) between the decrease in intensity of rsCDs (I0/I) with an increase in Cu2+ concentration was found in the concentration range of 0.27–3 mM (Fig. 3b). At Cu2+ concentration 2.66 mM, there was almost 60% quenching in the fluorescence intensity of rsCDs. We have further checked the quenching efficiency of various metals such as Mg2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ towards fluorescence of rsCDs (Fig. S2). It was seen that the quenching efficiency of both Fe3+ and Cu2+ was comparable. In the case of Fe(III), when we added the bilirubin, some precipitate was found. Hence Cu2+ was used as a quencher for further experiments. Hence we have taken 2.66 mM Cu2+ in further sensing experiments. The quenched fluorescence of the rsCDs-Cu2+ is recovered by adding BR. At optimal condition, the intensity of rsCDs-Cu2+ probe at different concentrations of BR is presented in Fig. 3c. In the range (0.7–3.6 μM), a linear relationship of fluorescence intensity to bilirubin concentration is achieved. The limit of detection is found to be 85 nM (Fig. 3d), which is comparable with other fluorimetric methods reported till now for the detection of bilirubin (Table 1 ). The influence of pH on the intensity of the emission band was investigated in order to determine the sensor's functional pH range. When the pH of the medium was 5, maximum turn-on response (F/F0) towards bilirubin was observed. Bilirubin competitively binds with Cu2+ ions in the rsCDs-Cu2+ complex by forming a more stable complex Bilirubin-Cu2+ complex. The interaction between Cu2+ ions and rsCDs gets weakened by the competitive coordination of the tetrapyrrole group and Cu2+ ions, hence restricting nonradiative energy transfer from rsCDs to Cu2+, and thus enhancing the fluorescence intensity.

The selectivity of our probe towards bilirubin was further examined in the presence of other biomolecules which are expected to be present in blood serum. Fig. 4 a shows an increase in fluorescence intensity of the rsCDs-Cu2+ probe in the presence of glucose, glutathione, urea, uric acid, glucosamine, fructose, bisphenol A and hemoglobin under similar experimental conditions (Figs. S3–S7). It had been found that in comparison to other molecules, the enhancement in FL intensity in the case of bilirubin was significantly high. The interference study was performed by adding bilirubin to rsCDs-Cu2+ in the presence of similar biomolecules and a larger anion, as illustrated in Fig. 4b. It was found that the existence of other molecules did not interfere in the recognition of bilirubin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and response time
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH on the detecting medium was investigated in order to determine the sensor's functioning pH range (Fig. S8a). When the pH of the medium is 5, the rsCDs-Cu2+ probe became more sensitive toward bilirubin. The response time of the sensing process was verified by taking 0.4 μM bilirubin at a different time of incubation. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the probe increased sharply after 20 min incubation (Fig. S8b).

A paper strip sensor was made by using Whatman 40 filter paper for detection of BR (Fig. 5 b). First, the solution of rsCDs-Cu2+ was dropped on a filter paper strip and then it was dried. After that, different concentration of bilirubin was dropped over the spot on the dried filter paper and dried again. Then the strip was visualized under day light as well as UV chamber of wavelength 365 nm. Under visible light, the color of the paper strip was yellow, but under UV (365 nm) it showed green luminescence. The change of color was studied by using a digital camera. The limit of detection for paper-based bilirubin sensing under UV (365 nm) was analysed using Image J software and found to be 2.4 μM (Fig. S9). As a result, it's evident that the paper strip can be utilised to identify BR.

As displayed in Fig. S10, a significant decrease in the UV absorption spectrum of rsCDs solution upon addition of Cu2+ and a clear redshift of 10 nm was observed, which could be ascribed to the formation of rsCDs-Cu2+, thus causing fluorescence quenching of rsCDs. The complexation of Cu2+ to rsCDs through surface functional groups is evidenced by XPS spectroscopy. The survey spectrum of rsCDs-Cu2+ indicates the presence of C1s, O1s and Cu(2p) (Fig. S11). The C1s spectrum indicates the presence of carbon in four different chemical environments such as CC, C–C, C–O and CO/COOH, corresponding to 284 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.5 eV, and 288.86 eV, respectively (Fig. 5a) which were in agreement with the appearance of IR stretching bands at 1200 (C–C), 1620(CC), and 1730 (CO) respectively. A high resolution scan of O1s depicted three peaks at 530, 532, and 533.21 eV associated with Cu–O, C–O and CO, respectively (Fig. 5b). High-resolution scan of the 2p region of C showed two clear peaks at 952.3 and 933eV with an energy gap of 19.3 eV which indicated the presence of Cu in Cu2+. Apart from that, there are several charge transfer satellite peaks at 941, 945 and 961 eV (Fig. 5c), which confirms the presence of Cu2+ complexed to surface oxygen-containing functional groups on rsCDs [38,39]. Furthermore, the excited state FL lifetime of rsCD was reduced from 1.91 nS to 1.65 nS after the addition of Cu2+ which evidenced the formation of rsCD-Cu2+ complex. The reduction in FL lifetime is attributed to the transfer of electron from the surface functional groups to the vacant d-orbital of Cu2+. The fluorescence quenching of rsCDs was maximum for Cu(II) as compared to other metals. Since the redox potential of Cu(II), Fe(III) and Ag(I) are positive, these ions assist the quenching based on electron transfer. The selectivity of the rsCDs towards Cu2+ over iron was due to its high electron affinity, which favours electron transfer. The fluorescence intensity of rsCDs was significantly increased in the presence of BR due to strong complexation of the tetrapyrrole ring with Cu2+ which is very sensible to coordinate with the metal ion. The stronger binding affinity between bilirubin and Cu2+ disturbs the nonfluorescent rsCDs-Cu2+ complex and the fluorescence intensity of rsCDs has recovered. To further corroborate our claim, we carried out a control experiment. When Cu2+ was added to CDs synthesized from sucrose and citric acid (scCD), there was much less quenching in the FL intensity in comparison to that of rsCDs. However, after the addition of BR to CD-Cu2+ FL intensity was not regained in a regular trend (Fig. S12). Hence it can be concluded that the complex formed between –phenolic OH of rsCDs and Cu2+ gets disturbed in the presence of bilirubin, whereas in case of sucrose derived CDs such kind of interaction is not observed.

To confirm the practicability of the sensor in the serum sample, we performed a sensing experiment in blood serums collected from a healthy person, jaundice patient and newborn babies. A proper dilution (90 times) was maintained to confirm that the concentration of bilirubin is in the dynamic range of the sensor. The results of our method for quantifying free bilirubin in serum samples agree with bilirubin measured with a hospital kit. The sensor's performance is excellent in all samples, as shown in Table 2 . In all concentration levels, the recovery percent of bilirubin in spiking serum and total blood samples varied from 93.75 to 97.14%, indicating no significant interference from other molecules for such treated samples. The aforesaid findings indicate that the developed bioassay has a lot of potential for detecting free bilirubin (FB) in biological fluids.

We have successfully developed fluorescent carbon dots from the hydrothermal decomposition of resorcinol and sucrose. The aqueous solution of rsCDs was yellow under visible light and intense green under UV light, with an PL quantum yield of 17.2%. The fluorescence of the rsCDs was quenched by adding copper ion and then addition of bilirubin restored the PL intensity with a detection limit of 85 nM. Furthermore, the designed fluorescence sensor was applied in real blood serum samples. In addition, such fluorescent carbon dots could have been spread on cellulose paper strip for visual detection of bilirubin with a limit of detection of 2.4 μM.
Balaram Barik: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Sasmita Mohapatra: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge SERB, DST, Govt. of India grant no CRG/2018/001717 for financial support for this work.
